title: Evaluation of the Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund, 2019-2020 to 2024-2025  - Canada.ca
content:
## Evaluation of the Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund, 2019-2020 to 2024-2025
Final Report – Executive Summary and Management Response and Action Plan October 2025 Prepared by the Office of Audit and Evaluation Public Health Agency of Canada
Note: The complete evaluation report is available upon request. Please send an email to oae-bae@phac-aspc.gc.ca .
- Executive summary Background and Evaluation Scope Key findings Recommendations
- Background and Evaluation Scope
- Management Response and Action Plan Recommendation 1 Recommendation 2

### Background and evaluation scope
Chronic diseases, such as diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer, are leading causes of death and illness in Canada. Addressing common risk factors, including tobacco use, unhealthy eating, and physical inactivity, can lower the likelihood of developing these diseases.
The Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund (HCCF) is a Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) funding program that seeks to prevent chronic disease among people living in Canada. Before the program’s renewal in 2020, it was known as the Healthy Living and Chronic Disease Prevention – Multi-Sectoral Partnerships (MSP) program. The program provides grants and contributions (G&Cs) to a variety of projects to address behavioural risk factors for chronic disease and to promote physical and social environments that support good health. Since its renewal, the HCCF has taken a health equity approach by focusing on priority populations that have been shown to face higher risks of acquiring chronic diseases. These include Indigenous Peoples, newcomers to Canada, people with disabilities, 2SLGBTQIA+ communities, people living on low incomes, and racialized communities. The program has an ongoing annual budget of approximately $25 million, including $22 million for G&Cs. Funding is provided to interventions based on three phases of project maturity:
- the Design Phase, intended for initial intervention design;
- the Implement Phase, intended for the delivery and evaluation of interventions; and
- the Scale Phase, intended for expanding successful interventions to achieve greater impact.
The purpose of the evaluation was to examine the program’s relevance, its effectiveness, and the strengths and challenges related to its design and delivery. It covered activities from fiscal years 2019-2020 to 2024-2025. The evaluation placed less emphasis on the performance of projects addressing tobacco use, as these were examined in a concurrent evaluation of the Health Portfolio’s Tobacco and Vaping Activities.

#### The HCCF is highly relevant and responsive to PHAC’s mandate and priorities. However, its flexible policy and financial authorities have also led to competing demands that create challenges in resource allocation.
As PHAC’s only continuous funding program for chronic disease prevention, the HCCF plays a significant role in meeting PHAC’s core responsibility for health promotion and chronic disease prevention. Its focus on priority populations also aligns with the Agency’s commitments to addressing health inequalities.
The HCCF is highly responsive to changing needs for chronic disease prevention, thanks to the broad policy and financial authorities that govern its spending. It is the largest health promotion funding program at PHAC that runs regular and open solicitations. At the same time, competing funding demands that make it difficult for the program to allocate its limited resources. In the past 6 years, approximately 12% of the program’s funding has been earmarked for, or redirected to emerging Agency priorities, some of which fall outside of the program’s objectives. The evaluation did not examine the performance of these projects. Internal G&Cs Modernization activities present opportunities to solidify the HCCF’s role in the broader portfolio of PHAC’s health promotion funding programs.

#### The HCCF complements other federal programs and policies, though there are opportunities for further linkages to gain efficiency and multiply impacts.
The HCCF’s activities complement policies and programs led by other federal departments by filling in funding gaps, targeting under-served populations, and supporting the testing and evaluation of promising approaches. The program explored collaborations across the federal government, including launching a joint solicitation with the Canadian Institutes for Health Research in 2021. Program partners highlighted how joint funding opportunities reduce administrative burden for applicants and funding recipients and mitigate potential duplication of effort. Further cross-departmental coordination could also allow the HCCF to leverage different ministerial mandates to address social or structural determinants of health that are outside of PHAC’s mandate, but which impact chronic disease prevention.

#### Recent program changes have increased the HCCF’s ability to address the needs of priority populations, though it has also decreased the program’s reach, as anticipated.
From 2019-2020 to 2024-2025, the HCCF reached 635,645 participants, nearly all from MSP projects. As program staff expected, HCCF Design Phase projects reported fewer participants than MSP projects as they targeted priority populations, which are harder to reach, and focused on testing and refining intervention designs rather than maximizing participation. Since the program’s renewal, the HCCF has introduced various measures to address the needs of priority populations, including increasing the focus of funding solicitations on addressing upstream factors of chronic disease and diversifying the types of projects and organizations that receive funding to better reach underserved populations.

#### Based on available data, the program met expectations for most medium- and long-term outcomes to improve participant health.
In the past 6 years, the program has generally met its targets to improve participant capacity to support healthy behaviours and prevent chronic diseases. However, results for improving participant knowledge have declined over time, falling outside the acceptable range in 2024-2025. The program has maintained consistent and satisfactory performance in improving participant health behaviours and their health status, in line with other regional or national programs. Results were mixed for improving participants’ environments to support ongoing healthy behaviours, though there was early evidence of improvements since program renewal. According to funding recipients, engaging priority populations, and forming multisectoral partnerships are the two most important facilitators of success.

#### HCCF program staff and funded projects collected and shared evidence of promising approaches for chronic disease prevention. Uptake of program-level knowledge mobilization products could be enhanced.
Most funded projects documented lessons learned and produced a considerable number of knowledge products. The HCCF also supported funding recipients by providing evaluation and knowledge mobilization resources and hosting peer knowledge-sharing sessions. At the program level, PHAC staff conducted and funded research studies, rolled up findings from funded projects, and produced and disseminated knowledge products, such as an interactive online map and lessons learned reports. Despite stakeholders expressing a desire for these kinds of products and activities, the evaluation found limited uptake, suggesting an opportunity to improve dissemination.

#### The HCCF has improved various aspects of its design and delivery as part of its renewal and in response to previous program reviews.
The transition from the MSP to the HCCF has led to many changes in how the program is managed. For example, the introduction of a phased funding model has allowed the program to target project funding at different stages of maturity. Meanwhile, the implementation of time-limited solicitations and an enhanced and continuous improvement process have enabled the program to adjust and improve itself between solicitation periods more effectively. The program has also amended aspects of its operations and governance in response to past evaluations and audits. Still, there remain areas for improvement in communicating feedback to funding applicants and the accessibility of application and reporting processes. The program’s ability to make changes is constrained by corporate requirements, although an internal G&Cs modernization initiative at PHAC presents some opportunities to address these challenges.

#### Matched funding requirements promoted sustainability and fostered multi-sectoral collaboration.
The HCCF program expects funding recipients to secure cash or in-kind funding from other sources equal to the amount received from PHAC. This matched funding requirement has encouraged funding recipients to form multisectoral partnerships and to sustain and amplify their project impact. Moreover, the program’s matched funding ratio and the average number of matched funding partners per funded project has improved over time thanks to new flexibilities introduced during the program’s renewal. The HCCF has not implemented other measures to promote project sustainability, although the evaluation identified multiple examples of projects that have continued their interventions after PHAC funding ended.

#### The program faced high demand for its G&Cs funding.
From 2019-2020 to 2024-2025, the HCCF had a budget of $149 million, including nearly $134 million in G&Cs funding. In this time, the program’s G&Cs funding envelope faced high demand. In the last two open solicitations, the HCCF funded 14% to 22% of eligible project applications. Moreover, in the last 6 fiscal years, approximately 12% of the program’s G&Cs funding was reallocated to other health promotion priorities at PHAC. After reallocations, the program spent within 3% of its remaining G&Cs budget each year. The remaining variance was due to administrative delays in approving funding dispersal and pandemic-related interruptions that hindered activities for certain projects.

#### Recommendation 1: Explore further collaborative funding opportunities with complementary federal programs.
The HCCF faces growing demands that make it difficult to allocate its existing resources to meet program objectives. At the same time, based on evidence of what works in chronic disease prevention, the program has expanded its activities to better address some of the underlying causes of chronic disease, such as healthy built environments and food security. Furthermore, other federal departments currently support the infrastructure costs required to effectively deliver program interventions in these spaces, which are not eligible expenditures under the HCCF’s current terms and conditions. There is an opportunity to better coordinate funding with other departments to maximize impact, achieve efficiency, reduce potential overlaps, and simplify the application process.

#### Recommendation 2: Identify strategies for improving knowledge dissemination and uptake.
Benefits of the program’s various knowledge mobilization efforts are not fully realized. Despite keen interest among stakeholders to access program evidence, there is limited awareness and uptake of current products and activities. It should be noted that the PHAC’s G&Cs Modernization initiative identifies a need for the development of a G&Cs knowledge mobilization strategy that is aligned with related PHAC initiatives. As such, any actions to improve HCCF’s knowledge mobilization will need to align with the PHAC-wide strategy.

## Management Response and Action Plan
Explore further collaborative funding opportunities with complementary federal programs.
Management agrees with the recommendation.
Expected Completion Date
1.1 PHAC will conduct a scan of G&Cs programs in the GoC to identify programs with complementary mandates and the potential for collaboration.

1.2 Based on scan results and emerging opportunities, PHAC will convene meetings with relevant departments to identify areas for potential collaboration including joint solicitations and reporting.
1.2.1 Departmental meeting schedules and agendas

Identify strategies for improving knowledge dissemination and uptake.
Management agrees with the recommendation.
Expected Completion Date
2.1 PHAC will conduct an environmental scan to identify best practices for knowledge dissemination and measurement of uptake.
2.1.1 Findings report
Executive Director, Centre for Chronic Disease Prevention and Health Equity
Internal human resources from the Programs and Performance division (PPD)
2.2 Based on the review, PHAC will develop a knowledge mobilization plan for the HCCF to strengthen uptake of knowledge products.
